title: MySQL database passwords should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: MySQL connection URL with embedded password detected. This connection string contains authentication credentials in plaintext format (mysql://user:password@host), providing direct database access. Exposing this allows unauthorized parties to execute queries, read sensitive data, modify records, and potentially compromise the entire database. Store credentials securely using environment variables or secrets management systems.

MySQL JDBC connection string with embedded password detected. JDBC URLs with password parameters (jdbc:mysql://host/db?password=xxx) expose database credentials in application code or configuration files. This grants full database access including read, write, and administrative operations. Remove passwords from connection strings and use secure credential management.

MySQL password detected in configuration. Plaintext database passwords in configuration files allow anyone with code access to authenticate to the database with full privileges. This enables unauthorized data exfiltration, modification, or deletion. Use environment variables, encrypted configuration, or secret management services instead.

MySQL password variable detected in source code. Hardcoded database passwords in source code can be easily discovered through code review, version control history, or decompilation. This provides direct access to production databases with full read/write capabilities. Store credentials in secure vaults or environment variables that are not committed to source control.
